text: After gaining abdominal access, the surgeon identifies the ovary and ureter, ensuring proper stent placement. The ovary is then suspended away from the operative field, exposing the broad ligament as the entry point into the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon parietalizes the peritoneum of the broad ligament to create the preperitoneal space, ensuring the correct plane is entered by confirming the integrity of the opposite side of the broad ligament.

The surgeon performs cautious dissection near the retroperitoneal vessels, including the internal iliac vein, with the sacrum visible in the surgical field. The sacrum is identified as a fixation point for the mesh.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the contour of the sciatic hernia. The contents of the hernia sac are reduced, and the hernia is shown with its contents fully reduced.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac vein and artery, with the sacrum fully dissected and visible, marking the posteromedial point for mesh fixation.
The fibers of the piriformis muscle are visible, serving as the posterolateral point for mesh fixation. The surgeon deploys a hybrid mesh composed of a permanent layer and an absorbable layer.

A large hybrid mesh is deployed over the dissected area, with the absorbable layer oriented medially to protect delicate retroperitoneal structures, including the sciatic nerve. The mesh will be trimmed to fit the dimensions of the dissection.

The surgeon interposes absorbable mesh between the ureter and permanent mesh to minimize the risk of ureteral erosion, adhesion, and obstruction. The permanent mesh is then sutured to the sacrum using IIO permanent suture, securing the mesh in place.
The surgeon fixes the mesh to the fibers of the piriformis muscle, completing the posterior edge fixation. The mesh is then trimmed to fit the pocket created during dissection.

The surgeon identifies the external obturator muscle as the anterolateral point of mesh fixation. The mesh is then secured to the levator ani muscles, marking the final anteromedial point of fixation.
The surgeon surveys the mesh to confirm its correct placement in the surgical space. Subsequently, the peritoneal defect is closed using a running barbed absorbable suture.
